result:
I've been thinking lately about the strange beauty of redundancy—how nature and human systems both seem to gravitate toward having backup plans for their backup plans.

Consider how we store memories. Not just in one neat filing cabinet in our minds, but scattered across neural networks, encoded in the emotions we felt, the scents we noticed, the songs that were playing. A single whiff of cinnamon can reconstruct an entire winter morning from decades past, complete with the texture of wool mittens and the sound of someone humming in the kitchen.

This redundancy appears everywhere once you start looking. Rivers carve multiple channels through deltas. Trees scatter thousands of seeds to ensure a few survive. We tell the same stories over and over, each telling slightly different, preserving the essence across generations even as the details shift like sand dunes.

Even in our digital age, where perfect reproduction seems possible, we create redundancy through variation—memes that mutate as they spread, cover songs that reinterpret originals, translations that become their own works of art. Perhaps there's something deep in the nature of information itself that resists being confined to a single, authoritative version.

It makes me wonder if consciousness itself might be redundant in ways we don't yet understand—not a single stream but multiple parallel processes, each creating its own version of "now," all somehow harmonizing into the singular experience of being you, reading these words, right at this moment.
